steps.prompt: Which word is a demonstrative pronoun?

hints.to eat: That would be 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.exsto, exstare, exstiti, —: These are the parts of 'exstare' (to stand out/exist)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'diligens' — 'dilectus' is perfect passive
hints.future active participle: Future active would be 'dilecturus' — 'dilectus' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'diligendus' — 'dilectus' is perfect passive participle

hints.deligo, deligere, delegi, delectum: These are the parts of 'deligere' (to choose/select)
hints.delecto, delectare, delectavi, delectatu: These are the parts of 'delectare' (to delight)
hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'legere' (to read/gather)

hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'Filii' — 'Filius' is nominative predicate
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Fili' — 'Filius' is predicate nominative with 'est'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'filii' ends in -i(i)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filii' ends in -i(i)
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'filii' ends in -i(i)
